title: Add test cases for login and logout
body:
As logout test request login to the registry, we plan to test them
together. There are five test cases added:
1. Podman login and logout with default value
3. Podman login and logout with --authfile
2. Podman login and logout with --tls-verify
4. Podman login and logout with --cert-dir
5. Podman login and logout with multi registry

All above test cases are using docker rgistry v2

comments.body: Looks like the new tests are failing?
Double checked the test logs and other pullreq test logs, seems the cert files has problem(expired). Will update them.
Still need make some update the tests for parallel testing. Will update it soon.
Looks like your tests are failing when running in rootless mode?
Needs a rebase, I think - we fixed these CI failures

Papr tests are falling over on all our PRs.
We have two remaining tests, both container-in-container testing...
I used to be the primary advocate for those being in place, but they've never revealed a problem that wasn't present on bare metal.
@baude @cevich What do you guys think about removing papr? Slims us down to just Openshift CI and Cirrus. If we have a demand for containerized testing, we can set it up in cirrus at a later date (and maybe do actual rootless container-in-container testing!)

@mheon I'm in favor of a simpler/slimmer overall CI setup.  I'm not certain exactly what code paths those tests exercise that are different from VM testing.  The atomic/coreos environment is super-duper difficult to orchestrate under Cirrus + Google, it's a SMOP.

Adding more VM-centric tasks into Cirrus is nearly laughably easy.  I would suggest we look into "Good Enough" alternatives for hitting the specific code-paths without the cost of an actual Atomic environment.  For example, are there some podman options we can set, then run a few specific sets of tests that would hit the important points, but use a full-OS VM instead?

Does continuing testing on Atomic make sense now that we're moving over to Fedora CoreOS upstream?

The original intent of the tests (when I asked for them) was to ensure that Podman in another container runtime, with privileged containers, didn't break. I think we can conclusively answer now that it doesn't really exercise Podman in a different way than running on bare metal. It's never found a break in the year and a half it's been up that the rest of CI didn't catch. I don't think OS really matters here.
@ypu if this PR is still needed, please rebase and we can perhaps watch it.
